main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Diffusion-model-guided Implicit Q-learning with Adaptive Revaluation (DIAR)","Use of diffusion models to learn state-action sequence distributions","Adaptive Revaluation mechanism for dynamic decision length adjustment","Alternating Q-function and value function learning from both dataset and diffusion model samples"],"problems":["Out-of-distribution sample handling in offline RL","Long-horizon decision-making in offline RL","Q-value overestimation in offline RL","Policy robustness and generalization in sparse-reward, long-horizon environments"],"datasets":["Maze2D","AntMaze","Kitchen"],"metrics":["Task success rate","Performance in long-horizon, sparse-reward environments"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance in Maze2D, AntMaze, and Kitchen","value":"DIAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of DIAR, a framework that integrates diffusion models with implicit Q-learning and adaptive revaluation for offline RL","Adaptive Revaluation mechanism that dynamically adjusts decision lengths by comparing current and future state values","Alternating training of Q-function and value function using both dataset and diffusion model samples","Demonstration of improved robustness and generalization in long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks compared to prior methods"]}

methods: Diffusion-model-guided Implicit Q-learning with Adaptive Revaluation (DIAR)
Use of diffusion models to learn state-action sequence distributions
Adaptive Revaluation mechanism for dynamic decision length adjustment
Alternating Q-function and value function learning from both dataset and diffusion model samples
problems: Out-of-distribution sample handling in offline RL
Long-horizon decision-making in offline RL

results.metric: Performance in Maze2D, AntMaze, and Kitchen
results.value: DIAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Introduction of DIAR, a framework that integrates diffusion models with implicit Q-learning and adaptive revaluation for offline RL
Adaptive Revaluation mechanism that dynamically adjusts decision lengths by comparing current and future state values
Alternating training of Q-function and value function using both dataset and diffusion model samples
Demonstration of improved robustness and generalization in long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks compared to prior methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Latent Diffusion-Constrained Q-learning (LDCQ)","State-conditioned latent diffusion modeling","Learning Q-function over states and latent skills","Batch-constraining candidate latents using diffusion prior","Policy decoder for low-level action sequence generation"],"problems":["Extrapolation error in offline reinforcement learning due to out-of-support queries","Difficulty in stitching suboptimal trajectory segments from static datasets","Poor credit assignment and slow reward propagation in long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks","Handling multi-modal data in offline RL"],"datasets":["D4RL benchmarks"],"metrics":["Standard D4RL benchmark scores (normalized scores)"],"results":[{"metric":"D4RL benchmark performance","value":"State-of-the-art; particularly excels in long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["First to leverage latent diffusion models for modeling in-support trajectory sequences as compressed latent skills in offline RL","Demonstrates that reasoning in a learned latent space enables richer task-specific information encoding and better credit assignment than raw state-action spaces","Separation of diffusion model and low-level policy allows use of powerful autoregressive decoders for action generation","Batch-constraining candidate latents using the diffusion prior effectively mitigates extrapolation error"]}

State-conditioned latent diffusion modeling
Learning Q-function over states and latent skills
Batch-constraining candidate latents using diffusion prior

Difficulty in stitching suboptimal trajectory segments from static datasets

novelty_claims: First to leverage latent diffusion models for modeling in-support trajectory sequences as compressed latent skills in offline RL

Batch-constraining candidate latents using the diffusion prior effectively mitigates extrapolation error

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Diffusion Q-learning (Diffusion-QL): representing the policy as a conditional diffusion model (denoising diffusion probabilistic model, DDPM)","Learning an action-value function (Q-function) and incorporating a policy improvement term into the diffusion model's training loss","Policy regularization via sample-based distribution matching using diffusion models"],"problems":["Offline reinforcement learning: learning optimal policies from static, previously collected datasets without environment interaction","Poor performance of standard RL methods in offline settings due to function approximation errors on out-of-distribution actions","Limited expressiveness of policy classes (e.g., Gaussian policies) leading to suboptimal solutions in policy regularization","Inadequate regularization methods (e.g., KL divergence, MMD) for offline RL"],"datasets":["D4RL benchmark tasks","Simple 2D bandit example with a multimodal behavior policy"],"metrics":["Task-specific performance metrics from D4RL (e.g., normalized score)","Comparison to state-of-the-art baselines"],"results":[{"metric":"D4RL benchmark performance","value":"Diffusion-QL achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of D4RL tasks"},{"metric":"2D bandit example","value":"Diffusion-QL outperforms prior methods in settings with multimodal behavior policies"}],"novelty_claims":["First to use diffusion models (DDPMs) as expressive policy classes for offline RL, enabling better modeling of complex, multimodal behavior policies","Introduces Diffusion-QL, which couples behavior cloning and policy improvement within the diffusion model framework for precise policy regularization","Demonstrates that diffusion models can serve as powerful sample-based policy regularizers without the need for extra behavior cloning steps","Injects Q-learning guidance into the reverse diffusion chain, allowing for iterative refinement towards optimal actions"]}

methods: Diffusion Q-learning (Diffusion-QL): representing the policy as a conditional diffusion model (denoising diffusion probabilistic model, DDPM)
Learning an action-value function (Q-function) and incorporating a policy improvement term into the diffusion model's training loss
Policy regularization via sample-based distribution matching using diffusion models
problems: Offline reinforcement learning: learning optimal policies from static, previously collected datasets without environment interaction
Poor performance of standard RL methods in offline settings due to function approximation errors on out-of-distribution actions
Limited expressiveness of policy classes (e.g., Gaussian policies) leading to suboptimal solutions in policy regularization
Inadequate regularization methods (e.g., KL divergence, MMD) for offline RL
datasets: Simple 2D bandit example with a multimodal behavior policy
metrics: Task-specific performance metrics from D4RL (e.g., normalized score)

results.value: Diffusion-QL achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of D4RL tasks
Diffusion-QL outperforms prior methods in settings with multimodal behavior policies

novelty_claims: First to use diffusion models (DDPMs) as expressive policy classes for offline RL, enabling better modeling of complex, multimodal behavior policies
Introduces Diffusion-QL, which couples behavior cloning and policy improvement within the diffusion model framework for precise policy regularization
Demonstrates that diffusion models can serve as powerful sample-based policy regularizers without the need for extra behavior cloning steps
Injects Q-learning guidance into the reverse diffusion chain, allowing for iterative refinement towards optimal actions

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Implicit Diffusion Q-learning (IDQL): a generalization of Implicit Q-learning (IQL) as an actor-critic method with a diffusion-based policy extraction scheme","Generalization of the IQL critic objective to arbitrary convex losses, inducing an implicit behavior-regularized actor","Policy extraction using samples from a diffusion-parameterized behavior policy, reweighted by critic-computed importance weights"],"problems":["Handling out-of-distribution actions in offline reinforcement learning (RL)","Accurately extracting policies from Q-functions trained with IQL, especially when the implicit actor is complex or multimodal","Improving the robustness and performance of offline RL methods, particularly with respect to hyperparameter sensitivity"],"datasets":["D4RL offline RL benchmarks (including antmaze and locomotion domains)"],"metrics":["Performance on D4RL benchmarks (normalized score)","Robustness to hyperparameters"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on D4RL benchmarks","value":"IDQL outperforms prior offline RL methods, including IQL and diffusion-based methods, across antmaze and locomotion tasks"},{"metric":"Robustness to hyperparameters","value":"IDQL demonstrates strong performance with limited hyperparameter tuning across domains"}],"novelty_claims":["Reinterpretation and generalization of IQL as an actor-critic method, where the critic loss induces an implicit actor distribution","Introduction of a diffusion-based policy extraction method that can accurately represent complex and multimodal implicit actor distributions","Demonstration that IDQL outperforms prior offline RL methods and is robust to hyperparameter choices, making it portable across domains"]}

methods: Implicit Diffusion Q-learning (IDQL): a generalization of Implicit Q-learning (IQL) as an actor-critic method with a diffusion-based policy extraction scheme
Generalization of the IQL critic objective to arbitrary convex losses, inducing an implicit behavior-regularized actor
Policy extraction using samples from a diffusion-parameterized behavior policy, reweighted by critic-computed importance weights

datasets: D4RL offline RL benchmarks (including antmaze and locomotion domains)

results.value: IDQL outperforms prior offline RL methods, including IQL and diffusion-based methods, across antmaze and locomotion tasks
IDQL demonstrates strong performance with limited hyperparameter tuning across domains

novelty_claims: Reinterpretation and generalization of IQL as an actor-critic method, where the critic loss induces an implicit actor distribution
Introduction of a diffusion-based policy extraction method that can accurately represent complex and multimodal implicit actor distributions
Demonstration that IDQL outperforms prior offline RL methods and is robust to hyperparameter choices, making it portable across domains

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Implicit Q-Learning (IQL): alternates between fitting an upper expectile value function using expectile regression and backing it up into a Q-function, then extracting the policy via advantage-weighted behavioral cloning; does not require querying or regularizing out-of-distribution actions"],"problems":["Offline reinforcement learning: learning effective policies from previously collected data without online interaction","Avoiding distributional shift and value function extrapolation when estimating values for unseen actions in offline RL","Improving policy performance beyond the behavior policy without querying out-of-distribution actions"],"datasets":["D4RL benchmark suite (including Ant Maze tasks)"],"metrics":["Normalized Score (as used in D4RL)"],"results":[{"metric":"Normalized Score (D4RL Ant Maze tasks)","value":"Significantly improves over prior state-of-the-art; achieves state-of-the-art performance on D4RL benchmark"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes implicit Q-learning (IQL), an offline RL algorithm that never queries values of unseen actions, avoiding the need for explicit constraints or regularization of out-of-distribution actions","Introduces the use of upper expectile regression for value function estimation in offline RL, enabling in-sample learning only","Demonstrates that IQL achieves state-of-the-art performance on D4RL, especially on challenging tasks requiring trajectory stitching","Shows that IQL is suitable for fine-tuning with online interaction after offline initialization"]}

methods: Implicit Q-Learning (IQL): alternates between fitting an upper expectile value function using expectile regression and backing it up into a Q-function, then extracting the policy via advantage-weighted behavioral cloning; does not require querying or regularizing out-of-distribution actions

results.value: Significantly improves over prior state-of-the-art; achieves state-of-the-art performance on D4RL benchmark

novelty_claims: Proposes implicit Q-learning (IQL), an offline RL algorithm that never queries values of unseen actions, avoiding the need for explicit constraints or regularization of out-of-distribution actions

Develops an efficient behavior policy to reduce policy variance and improve sample efficiency in online RL with diffusion models.
First to achieve state-of-the-art performance with diffusion-based policies in online RL on MuJoCo benchmarks.

methods: Policy in the Latent Action Space (PLAS): learning the policy in the latent space of a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) trained on the behavior policy, then decoding latent actions to environment actions
Implicit constraint of policy actions to the support of the dataset via the latent action space, instead of explicit constraints (e.g., KL or MMD divergence)
Augmentation to allow controlled out-of-distribution actions when Q-function generalizes well
problems: Offline reinforcement learning from static datasets without further environment interaction
Extrapolation error in Q-function due to out-of-distribution actions in static datasets
Constraining policy actions to the support of the dataset without being overly restrictive
metrics: Task performance (e.g., normalized score on d4rl benchmarks)

results.value: PLAS outperforms existing offline RL methods with explicit constraints and provides competitive performance across various tasks and datasets

novelty_claims: Proposes learning the policy in the latent action space of a CVAE trained on the behavior policy, which implicitly constrains actions to the support of the dataset
Avoids the need for explicit action distribution matching constraints (e.g., KL or MMD)
Demonstrates that this approach provides consistently good performance across diverse datasets and tasks, including real-robot experiments
Introduces a method to allow controlled out-of-distribution actions for improved performance when Q-function generalization is sufficient

methods: Decision Transformer: casting reinforcement learning as conditional sequence modeling using a causally masked Transformer (GPT-style)
Autoregressive modeling of trajectories (sequences of returns-to-go, states, and actions)
Conditioning on desired return to generate actions that achieve specified rewards
problems: Reinforcement learning from offline data (offline RL)
Long-term credit assignment in RL without dynamic programming or bootstrapping
Learning effective policies from suboptimal, fixed datasets
Generalization and transfer in RL via sequence modeling
metrics: Comparison to state-of-the-art model-free offline RL baselines

results.metric: Performance on Atari, OpenAI Gym, and Key-to-Door (average episode return)
results.value: Matches or exceeds state-of-the-art model-free offline RL baselines

results.metric: Performance on long-term credit assignment tasks
results.value: Outperforms RL baselines

novelty_claims: First to cast reinforcement learning as a conditional sequence modeling problem using transformers, rather than value function or policy gradient methods
Demonstrates that a simple autoregressive transformer can match or exceed state-of-the-art offline RL algorithms without dynamic programming or bootstrapping
Shows that conditioning on desired return enables flexible policy generation and skill specification
Avoids the need for discounting and Bellman backups, enabling direct credit assignment via self-attention

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Conservative Normalizing Flows (CNF): a method that constructs a latent action space for offline RL using a Normalizing Flows-based action encoder, pre-trained on the offline dataset, and a controller trained in the latent space via RL"],"problems":["Extrapolation error in offline RL due to value estimation of out-of-distribution state-action pairs","Distributional shift between behavior and inference policies in offline RL","Instability and ad-hoc solutions (e.g., latent space clipping) in previous latent action space methods (e.g., VAE-based PLAS)"],"datasets":["D4RL locomotion datasets","D4RL maze2d datasets"],"metrics":["Standard RL evaluation metrics (e.g., average episode return, normalized score) as used in D4RL benchmarks"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes the use of Normalizing Flows (NFs) to construct a conservative latent action space for offline RL, avoiding the need for manual latent space clipping required by VAE-based methods.","Introduces an architectural modification to NFs that prevents extrapolation error without discarding in-dataset actions.","Demonstrates that the CNF method outperforms previous generative action model-based algorithms on a large portion of D4RL locomotion and maze2d datasets."]}

methods: Conservative Normalizing Flows (CNF): a method that constructs a latent action space for offline RL using a Normalizing Flows-based action encoder, pre-trained on the offline dataset, and a controller trained in the latent space via RL
problems: Extrapolation error in offline RL due to value estimation of out-of-distribution state-action pairs
Distributional shift between behavior and inference policies in offline RL
Instability and ad-hoc solutions (e.g., latent space clipping) in previous latent action space methods (e.g., VAE-based PLAS)
metrics: Standard RL evaluation metrics (e.g., average episode return, normalized score) as used in D4RL benchmarks
novelty_claims: Proposes the use of Normalizing Flows (NFs) to construct a conservative latent action space for offline RL, avoiding the need for manual latent space clipping required by VAE-based methods.
Introduces an architectural modification to NFs that prevents extrapolation error without discarding in-dataset actions.
Demonstrates that the CNF method outperforms previous generative action model-based algorithms on a large portion of D4RL locomotion and maze2d datasets.

methods: SPiRL (Skill-Prior RL): a deep latent variable model that jointly learns an embedding space of skills and a prior over skills from unstructured agent experience
Extension of maximum-entropy RL algorithms to incorporate learned skill priors for downstream task learning
problems: How to leverage large amounts of unstructured prior agent experience to accelerate reinforcement learning on new tasks
How to efficiently transfer and utilize large libraries of learned skills for downstream RL tasks without suffering from inefficient exploration in large skill spaces
datasets: Simulated navigation tasks (specific environments not named)
Simulated robotic manipulation tasks (specific environments not named)
metrics: Learning efficiency (e.g., speed of learning new tasks)

results.value: SPiRL accelerates learning of new tasks compared to baselines
Learned skill priors enable higher success rates in downstream tasks

novelty_claims: Joint learning of a skill embedding space and a prior over skills from unstructured data

Demonstration that learned skill priors are essential for effective skill transfer and accelerated learning from rich datasets

methods: OPAL (Offline Primitives for Accelerating offline reinforcement Learning): an unsupervised learning method to extract temporally extended primitive behaviors from offline data, which are then used as a constrained action space for downstream offline RL policy optimization
Incorporation of OPAL as a preprocessing step for standard online RL, few-shot imitation learning, and multi-task transfer learning
problems: How to leverage large, undirected, and unlabeled offline datasets to improve offline reinforcement learning
How to avoid distributional shift and compounding errors in offline RL
How to provide temporal abstraction to reduce the effective horizon in offline RL
How to accelerate few-shot imitation learning, exploration, and transfer in RL using offline data
datasets: Maze navigation environment (offline datasets of undirected navigation)
Benchmark domains for offline RL (not explicitly named in the provided text)
metrics: Offline RL policy performance (e.g., task success rate, reward)
Speed and quality of downstream task learning

results.metric: Offline RL policy performance (CQL+OPAL vs. CQL)
results.value: CQL+OPAL achieves significantly improved performance over CQL alone on both medium and large datasets (as shown in Figure 55, though exact numbers are not provided in the text)

results.metric: Speed and quality of downstream task learning
results.value: OPAL improves both the speed and quality of learning in online RL, few-shot imitation, and multi-task transfer settings

novelty_claims: First work to theoretically justify and experimentally verify the benefits of primitive learning in offline RL settings

Demonstrates that hierarchies (via primitive learning) provide temporal abstraction that reduces compounding errors in offline RL, in contrast to prior work in online RL where exploration was the main benefit
Shows that unsupervised primitive extraction from offline data can be used to accelerate not only offline RL but also online RL, few-shot imitation, and transfer learning

methods: Standard online off-policy RL algorithms for finetuning (e.g., TD3)
Conservative policy optimization procedure for stable and sample-efficient online learning
Constrained policy optimization extension to TD3 (TD3-C)
problems: How to effectively finetune agents obtained from offline RL training with additional online data
Slow improvement of online performance when using offline RL algorithms for finetuning

Trade-offs in algorithm choices and use of offline data during online finetuning
novelty_claims: Analysis of the challenges and trade-offs in finetuning offline RL agents with online data
Empirical investigation of policy collapse during online finetuning and its relation to data diversity, algorithm choice, and replay distribution
